planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost, or root stem cuttings in late summer for overwintering; blue marguerite blooms most freely in cool weather and may slow down in the hottest midsummer weeks. It is perennial in zones 9–11 where it can persist for several years as a small, sub-shrubby plant; in colder zones treat as a tender annual. Deadhead regularly to prolong bloom.

directions: Start seeds early indoors or plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; blue marguerite is a South African native producing one of the most reliably cheerful and saturated sky-blue daisy flowers available in cool-season gardening — a compact, bushy, small shrubby plant covered from spring through summer (and in mild climates through winter and fall) with 1–1.5 inch daisy flowers in clear, true, vivid sky-blue with a small golden-yellow central disc. The specific blue is particularly valuable in horticulture — a clean, medium blue that photographs accurately and provides a reliable complementary contrast to orange, yellow, and golden flowers in mixed plantings. It performs best in cool to moderate temperatures and may reduce flowering in the hottest midsummer weeks in humid climates. Blue marguerite is particularly valuable in the Gulf Coast and mild Pacific Coast winter gardens, where it blooms prolifically through fall and mild winters. Variegated-leaf cultivars ('Variegata') are also available. Outstanding in containers, where it can be overwintered indoors in cold climates and moved outdoors in summer.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring or late summer in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; rose-scented geranium is perennial in zones 9–11 and grows as a sprawling, scented shrub in mild climates. In colder zones bring indoors in winter. The leaves and stems are the commercial source of geranium essential oil (often labeled rose geranium oil) used in perfumery, cosmetics, and aromatherapy.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile to lean soil in full sun; rose-scented geranium is a South African coastal dune native and the primary commercial source of geranium essential oil — one of the most important fragrance materials in perfumery, used to round out and fix rose notes in countless fine fragrances. The deeply lobed, velvety, gray-green leaves release an immediately recognizable, rich, rosy-citrusy fragrance when touched — often described as a green rose or rose combined with mint — that is more complex and interesting than an actual rose. The small, pink, pelargonium-type flowers appear in clusters through the growing season but are secondary to the extraordinary aromatic foliage. In zones 9–11 it grows as a substantial, spreading sub-shrub. The oil is extracted by steam distillation of the green stems and leaves; commercial production centers on the island of Réunion and South Africa. Excellent as a container herb for patios where the fragrant foliage can be brushed when passing. Dried leaves retain fragrance well for sachets and potpourri.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring or late summer in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; nutmeg geranium is perennial in zones 9–11 and can be overwintered indoors as a houseplant in colder zones. It is compact enough for windowsill culture and is one of the most commonly grown and shared scented-leaf geraniums in cottage and herb gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile to lean soil in full sun; nutmeg geranium is a compact hybrid scented-leaf pelargonium with small, roundish, slightly gray-green, softly hairy leaves that release an unmistakable warm, spicy nutmeg-and-pine fragrance when touched or rubbed — quite different from the rosy fragrance of P. capitatum or the lemony notes of P. crispum. The fragrance is immediately recognizable as nutmeg with a fresh, slightly turpentine-woody undertone. Small, white, pink-veined pelargonium flowers appear in spring and early summer. A traditional cottage and kitchen garden herb grown for centuries for the use of its fragrant leaves in cooking (particularly in pound cakes, custards, and sugar syrups where a leaf placed in the bottom of the pan imparts its fragrance), for herbal sachets, and as a fragrant window-sill plant. Very easy to grow from cuttings, which root readily and were traditionally passed between neighbors and friends — the most common mode of propagation for all scented geraniums, which are grown almost exclusively as cutting-propagated plants rather than from seed.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring or late summer in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; oak-leaf geranium grows into a large, woody shrub in zones 9–11, reaching 4–5 feet or more in warm climates. Prune back hard each spring to maintain compact, vigorous growth and fresh aromatic foliage.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; oak-leaf geranium is a boldly distinctive scented-leaf pelargonium species prized for two features — its unusually large, deeply lobed, oak-leaf-shaped leaves (which give the plant its species name quercifolium, Latin for 'oak-leaved') and the strong, pungent, bitter-almond to balsamic fragrance released when the sticky, glandular leaves are handled, distinctly different from the rose or citrus notes of other scented pelargoniums. The leaves are large and rough-textured compared to most pelargoniums, sticky to the touch, and the plant becomes a large, somewhat gawky but aromatic shrub in warm climates. Small rose-pink to magenta flowers appear in spring and summer. Used in the fragrance industry and in potpourri for its balsamic notes. In zones 9–11 it can grow into an impressive specimen shrub with an overwhelming presence when the foliage is disturbed in the breeze. 'Fair Ellen' is a widely available cultivar with particularly deep-cut, decorative leaves. A plant grown more for fragrant-foliage effect than ornamental flowers — one of the most botanically interesting of the diverse scented pelargonium group.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds where they are to grow 4–6 weeks before last frost in cool weather (or in fall in mild climates); Virginia stock matures so quickly — as little as 4–5 weeks from sowing to first flower — that successive sowings every 3–4 weeks from early spring through early summer and again in early fall extend the season. It does not transplant well and should always be direct-sown where it is to grow.

directions: Direct sow in cool weather in full sun in any moderately fertile, well-drained soil; Virginia stock (despite its common name, a Mediterranean native unrelated to American Virginia) is one of the fastest-flowering annuals in existence — germinating in less than a week and producing flowers within 4–5 weeks of sowing, making it ideal for impatient gardeners, children's gardens, and gaps in the border that need instant color. The flowers are small (half an inch), simple four-petaled cruciform blooms in the traditional cross shape of the Brassicaceae family, in cheerful shades of lilac, pink, red, white, and yellow — all usually sold as a mixed color packet. The plants grow quickly, flower prolifically, set seed, and die within their first season, but self-sow freely to maintain colonies. The flowers have a light, sweet, clove-like fragrance. Excellent for filling gaps rapidly in mixed beds, cottage-garden style plantings, and children's gardens where the fast germination-to-flower timeline provides immediate satisfaction. Sow in broad sweeps for the best effect; individual small plants can look weedy but mass sowings create cheerful carpets of color.
